---
title: "Hash functions"
subtitle: "Why Aleo programs use BHP and Poseidon, not SHA-256."
---

::: {.callout-note}
**Status:** Stub. Full chapter coming soon.
:::

## The question

In normal software, you reach for SHA-256 or Keccak when you need a hash.
In Leo, the canonical choice is `BHP256` or `Poseidon4`. Why? Are SHA-256
and Keccak even available?

## What this chapter will cover

- The "ZK-friendly hash" concept: why hash choice matters when the hash is computed *inside a ZK proof*
- Cost comparison: number of circuit constraints for each hash
  - SHA-256: ~25,000 constraints per block
  - Keccak: ~150,000 constraints per block
  - Poseidon4: ~200 constraints per hash
  - BHP256: ~1,000 constraints
- Aleo's full hash menu: BHP, Pedersen, Poseidon (×3 variants), SHA-3, Keccak
- When to use which:
  - Hashing addresses → BHP256
  - Inside a Merkle tree → Poseidon4
  - Compatibility with EVM signatures → Keccak256
  - Standardization or external verification → SHA-3

## Interactive viz planned

A bar chart: pick a hash, see the constraint cost as a stack of unit squares.
Slider for input size. Compare 6 hash families side-by-side.

## Why this matters

Picking the wrong hash can 100× your transaction's proving time. The Merkle
tree chapter you just (or about to) read uses Poseidon4 *because* it's so
cheap inside a circuit. SHA-256 in the same role would make the proof
generation impractical.
